analysis_sections.f1_profile:
## Aljamain Sterling -- Style & Profile

- Sterling is a clinch-first, control-oriented fighter who has built his career on making fights ugly — he smothers opponents at the fence, limits their offense, and grinds out decisions rather than hunting finishes. His last ten fights produced only one KO and one submission, with five decisions. That's not an accident; it's a blueprint.
- His striking numbers tell an interesting story: 4.26 significant strikes landed per minute for his career, but his last three fights show a dramatic collapse — 0.32, 1.08, and 0.20 strikes per minute respectively. That's not a slump, that's a fundamental shift in how he's fighting. He's become almost exclusively a grapple-and-survive operator at featherweight.
- His striking accuracy has fallen off a cliff in recent work. Career average is 52%, but his last two fights against Zalal and Evloev came in at 33% and 27% respectively. He's not landing clean, and when you're already not a volume striker, that's a problem.
- His takedown defense sits at just 44% for his career — well below the threshold where a striker can feel safe against a grappler. The irony is that Sterling is himself a grappler, yet he gets taken down when opponents commit to it.
- At 36, he's at the age where fighters in his weight class typically begin their measurable decline. His KO loss to Sean O'Malley in 2023 — stopped in 51 seconds — was a signal worth noting. He took that fight at bantamweight; he's now competing at 145, suggesting his body has genuinely moved up, not that he's a natural featherweight with extra margin.
- His win over Zalal in April 2026 came at 0.32 strikes per minute — one of the lowest-output performances you'll see from a main event fighter. He survived more than he competed. That line between survival and dominance is getting thinner.
analysis_sections.f2_profile:
## Youssef Zalal -- Style & Profile

- Zalal is a submission hunter first, a grappler second, and a striker when he has to be. Four of his last five wins have come by submission — rear naked choke finishes across the board. His submission average of 1.3 attempts per 15 minutes is the clearest signal in his profile: he wants the fight on the ground and he wants to end it there.
- His striking output against Sterling in their first fight was virtually zero — 0.16 strikes per minute with 25% accuracy. That's not because he can't strike; against Calvin Kattar he posted 1.73 strikes per minute at 56.5% accuracy. Against Sterling specifically, his offense was neutralized entirely.
- The Josh Emmett win was the most statistically extreme performance in his recent history: 9.18 takedowns per 15 minutes and 9.18 submission attempts per 15 minutes in a first-round finish. That kind of output in a short burst tells you what Zalal looks like when he gets the fight exactly where he wants it — it's decisive.
- His strike defense sits at 64% career average — meaningfully better than Sterling's offensive output in their rematch context. He absorbs only 1.79 strikes per minute, which means he's not getting beaten up standing, he's just not winning there either.
- At 29 and on a four-fight win streak with a draw before that, Zalal is in the ascending portion of his career curve while Sterling is likely past his. The age gap here is seven years — that's not insignificant in a five-round fight.
- His takedown defense of 55% is his biggest vulnerability. Sterling is a career grappler who will look to hold position, and Zalal's inability to stop takedowns is why their first fight went the way it did — Zalal posted zero takedowns and zero submission attempts across 25 minutes.
analysis_sections.head2head:
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

We already have one data point from this exact matchup: Sterling won a unanimous decision in April 2026, and he did it almost entirely by making the fight nothing. Both men combined for fewer than three significant strikes per minute across five rounds. Sterling clinched, controlled, and denied Zalal the scrambles and submission attempts that define his game. Zalal's 55% takedown defense was the master variable — Sterling pressed him against the fence repeatedly, Zalal couldn't reverse or drag Sterling down on his own terms, and the result was 25 minutes of suffocating anti-action.

The core problem for Zalal in a rematch is structural: his path to victory requires getting Sterling to the floor in a scramble he can exploit, but Sterling's entire game plan is built around denying that. Sterling doesn't need to strike — he just needs to be heavier, more controlling, and more willing to win ugly. And he's proven he can do exactly that. Zalal's striking, which is his only real alternative path, produced nothing against Sterling's 59% career strike defense and his tendency to close distance immediately. Where Zalal hurt Emmett and Kattar with takedowns and transitions, Sterling was the one doing the clinching and the smothering.

The legitimate counter-argument for Zalal is the five-round format and the age gap. Sterling is 36 and his output numbers across the last three fights suggest he's operating at reduced capacity — particularly in a fight he's already won, the motivation to take risks and open up may be even lower. If Zalal can find one scramble, one clean takedown where Sterling doesn't fully settle into control, the submission threat is real — he's finished four of his last five wins by submission and Sterling's submission defense (career sub-defense is unmeasured here, but he has been submitted before). The net intangible picture slightly favors Zalal on the age dimension, but Sterling's demonstrated ability to shut this specific opponent down in 25 minutes is a very heavy weight on the other side of the scale.
analysis_sections.endings:
## Most Likely Fight Endings

**#1 -- Aljamain Sterling wins by unanimous decision, Round 5**
Likelihood: High
Why: The blueprint already exists — Sterling clinches, controls the fence, and grinds Zalal into passivity. In their first meeting, Zalal was held to zero takedowns and zero submission attempts across 25 minutes. Sterling doesn't need to strike cleanly or finish; he just needs to deny Zalal the scrambles that make him dangerous. Unless something changes in the rematch preparation, the structural read is the same fight playing out the same way.

**#2 -- Youssef Zalal wins by submission, Rounds 2–4**
Likelihood: Moderate-Low
Why: Zalal's only path is catching Sterling in a scramble where the control grip breaks. He's finished four straight opponents this way, and Sterling — despite his grappling background — has been submitted before and is moving up a weight class later in his career. If Zalal adjusts his approach and finds even one clean entry, his finishing instinct and submission rate are legitimate enough to end the fight quickly. The longer this fight goes, the more a tire-and-catch scenario benefits the 29-year-old.

**#3 -- Youssef Zalal wins by unanimous decision, Round 5**
Likelihood: Low
Why: If Zalal can successfully adjust his distance management and use his slight reach advantage to keep Sterling from clinching freely, the striking numbers favor Zalal on accumulation — his defense is better and Sterling's output has declined sharply. This is unlikely given how thoroughly Sterling neutralized him in Fight 1, but if Sterling's physical capacity has diminished further since April 2026, a judges' decision could flip on volume.

**#4 -- Aljamain Sterling wins by submission, Rounds 1–3**
Likelihood: Low
Why: Sterling has 0.6 submission attempts per 15 minutes for his career and one submission win in his last ten fights, but he does have an elite grappling background and if he secures deep back control from a clinch position, the finish is always possible. Given his low output in recent fights, this feels like a stretch — but it's not zero.
analysis_sections.betting:
**Tier: NO BET**

**Primary Pick: PASS**

This is a rematch of a fight that already happened six days ago — the same two fighters, the same weight class, the same five-round format. Sterling won the first meeting by unanimous decision and the line has moved dramatically from Sterling -155 to essentially a pick'em, with Sterling now listed around -105 and Zalal at +135 depending on the book. That's a massive shift toward Zalal, but it's not reverse line movement — both lines have lengthened, which means the action has been two-sided rather than sharp money hammering one side.

The core issue with betting Sterling is that his assessed probability of winning a rematch six days later is meaningfully higher than the -105 line implies — he just beat this exact opponent using a game plan that Zalal showed no ability to counter over 25 minutes. Sterling's clinch-control approach is repeatable and doesn't require him to be physically dominant the way he would need to be at, say, 35 striking strikes per minute. The case for Sterling is essentially "he just won and nothing has changed in six days."

The case against Sterling — and why the line has moved — is age (36, at the cliff for his weight class), dramatically reduced output in his last three fights, a history that includes a 51-second KO loss, and the reality that a 29-year-old in peak form has a legitimate physical upside edge in the later rounds of a 25-minute fight. Zalal at +135 is not crazy value on its face.

But here's the problem: both fighters just competed six days ago. Meaningful preparation changes are essentially impossible. Zalal cannot have materially altered his game plan, his conditioning, or his technical approach to the specific problems Sterling presents. Sterling cannot have fixed whatever made his output so low — nor does he need to. The fight as a betting market is priced as though we're uncertain about the outcome, but the evidence we have is a 25-minute fight from six days ago that Sterling won clearly enough to go 5-0 on the judges' scorecards (a unanimous decision).

The line movement away from Sterling likely reflects public reaction to the mismatch in stylistic entertainment value, the legitimate concern about Sterling's age and declining output, and some uncertainty about why a rematch was booked this quickly. None of that changes the analytical read. Sterling's path to winning is proven and intact; Zalal's path requires something he couldn't find in 25 minutes last week.

With no live market odds to de-vig and compute a precise edge, and with both fighters coming off a fight six days ago in unusual rematch circumstances, the appropriate call is to pass. The market data unavailable note applies to precise Kelly sizing. The read points toward Sterling, but without verified current lines across books to confirm the implied probability gap, committing units to this is analytically unsound.

**Stake: 0 units**
**Target line: Sterling no worse than -130 if market data becomes available**
**Data tiers: Sterling — career stats present but last-3 sample is concerning; Zalal — similar situation. Both treated as medium-tier given the unusual 6-day rematch context.**
**Market read: Opening lines were Sterling -155 / Zalal +115. Both have lengthened to approximately pick'em territory. No confirmed reverse line movement — movement appears two-sided. No public percentage data available.**

**Hedge verdict: NO ARB** — With live lines unavailable, a proper cross-book arb calculation cannot be run. The line movement suggests the market has re-centered toward a coin flip, but without verified current book-by-book lines, no arb can be confirmed or sized.

**Props worth considering:** If forced to find value, the "fight goes to decision" prop — if available — should have the highest probability of any outcome given that their first meeting went five rounds at near-zero combined output. A decision prop at reasonable juice would likely have positive expected value based on both fighters' recent patterns, but without a verified line to de-vig, no specific recommendation can be made.

analysis_sections.f1_profile:
## Joselyne Edwards -- Style & Profile

- Edwards is an orthodox striker who outputs at a solid 4.61 significant strikes per minute across her UFC career, but her last three fights show wildly inconsistent volume — she threw almost nothing in the Cornolle and first Dumont fights, then went nuclear (10.83/min) against Cachoeira in a R1 KO. She adapts her output to the opponent rather than imposing a pace.
- Her 53% striking accuracy is legitimately good for the division, and she carries real finishing power — two KO wins and two submission wins in her last ten fights suggest she can close the show when she gets an opening, not just grind out decisions.
- Defensively, her 50% strike defense is below average, meaning she eats roughly half the strikes thrown at her. She's not a counter-fighter built on head movement — she tends to win through output and pressure rather than slipping and shooting back.
- Her takedown game is a secondary weapon rather than a primary one: 1.29 attempts per 15 minutes, landing at only 36%. She doesn't go to the ground to finish — her submissions come from opportunistic scrambles, not a wrestling-based system.
- Three weight misses in her fight history is a meaningful red flag. Her body clearly struggles with the cut at times, which can translate into energy issues later in fights. Worth noting she won a three-round decision in fight #1 against Dumont on six days' rest — the same turnaround she's on now.
- Her five-fight winning streak includes wins of varying quality: a KO over a durable Cachoeira, a submission over Cornolle (who had beaten her before), and a decision over Dumont just six days ago. The momentum is real and recent.
analysis_sections.f2_profile:
## Norma Dumont -- Style & Profile

- Dumont is one of the cleaner technical strikers in the women's bantamweight division, landing at 50% accuracy while absorbing only 2.33 strikes per minute — her 64% strike defense is among the best in the weight class. She wins by not getting hit, not by overwhelming opponents.
- Eight decisions in her last eight fights tell you everything about her style: she's a point-fighter who controls range, picks her shots, and frustrates opponents into grinding losses. She has zero submission attempts across her entire UFC career and zero KO wins.
- The caveat to her clean defensive numbers is that her output has been declining. She landed 2.13/min against Aldana in September 2024, dropped to 1.13 against Vieira, and then managed just 0.40 against Edwards in the fight six days ago — a fight she lost. Her last performance was her worst statistically.
- At 35 years old with five career weight misses, Dumont carries real risk around weight cuts and their downstream effects on her sharpness and output. Five misses is a significant track record of body-weight management problems.
- Her grappling is underrated — 54% takedown accuracy and 71% TD defense means she can both threaten on the feet without worrying about being taken down easily. She doesn't use wrestling offensively in recent fights (0.0 TD/15 in her last three) but she has the tools.
- She already lost to Edwards once, six days ago, and now has to come back and fight her again on a week's notice. The psychological and physical reset from a decision loss on that timeline is a genuine concern that the stats alone don't fully capture.
analysis_sections.head2head:
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

This fight already happened six days ago, and Edwards won a unanimous decision. The rematch question is whether Dumont can make meaningful adjustments in less than a week — and the honest answer is probably not. Edwards has a 3-inch reach advantage and is the more physically active fighter, and in their first meeting, Dumont couldn't find an answer for it. Both fighters managed just 0.40 significant strikes per minute in that bout, which tells you the pace was deliberately low, and in a low-output technical fight, Edwards' aggression and reach gave her the edge on the cards.

Dumont is the better pure technician on paper — her 64% strike defense is significantly cleaner than Edwards' 50%, and if she can establish range and use movement to force Edwards into missed exchanges, she can steal rounds on the judges' cards. Her path to winning this rematch runs through staying disciplined, not getting caught reaching, and using her footwork to make Edwards miss early. The problem is she couldn't execute that in fight one, and at 35 on six days of recovery from a loss, expecting a fundamentally different version of Dumont is optimistic.

Edwards' clearest advantage beyond the reach differential is her finishing ability. Dumont has never been stopped in her UFC career, which limits the KO/sub paths, but Edwards doesn't need to finish — she just needs to do what she already did. She pressures forward, lands cleaner in exchanges, and her 5-inch age advantage means the physical output gap that existed in fight one is unlikely to close in a week. The net intangible picture here slightly favors Edwards: she's younger, she already solved this matchup once, and she's shown finishing upside if Dumont slows down.
analysis_sections.endings:
## Most Likely Fight Endings

**#1 -- Joselyne Edwards wins by unanimous decision**
Likelihood: High
Why: The most recent data point we have is Edwards winning by unanimous decision over this exact opponent six days ago. Neither fighter has ever finished the other, Dumont has never been stopped in the UFC, and both are operating on minimal recovery time. A second gritty, technical decision with Edwards controlling the pace and reach advantage is the most logical projection.

**#2 -- Norma Dumont wins by split or unanimous decision**
Likelihood: Moderate
Why: Dumont is the more technically sound striker, and close fights go to the judges. If she makes minor adjustments — staying more active on the outside, using her footwork to control range, and forcing Edwards to chase — she can steal two rounds on a close card. Their first fight was clearly competitive enough that a second judges' verdict going the other way isn't far-fetched.

**#3 -- Joselyne Edwards wins by TKO/KO**
Likelihood: Low
Why: Edwards has real finishing power and can end fights when opponents slow down, as she showed against Cachoeira. Dumont has never been stopped in the UFC, but if the weight cut, the short turnaround, and the physical load of fight one accumulate, she could be more hittable than usual in the later rounds. It's not a likely outcome but it's in play given Edwards' proven finishing ability.

The market has moved significantly toward Dumont — she opened around +195 and is now sitting near -200, while Edwards went from +170 to +165. That's a massive shift on Dumont without confirmed reverse line movement driving it. The public appears to be reacting to a rematch scenario by assuming Dumont will correct the loss, which is a common narrative trap. Edwards won the fight six days ago and is now being offered as a moderate underdog.

**The case:**
Edwards solved this matchup once already. She has a 3-inch reach advantage, is five years younger, outputs at a higher rate, and has finishing ability Dumont simply doesn't possess. Dumont's output in fight one was the worst of her last three fights (0.40 SLpM), and she's coming back on six days' rest after a loss. The line moving almost 400 cents toward Dumont on a rematch she just lost — without an obvious sharp or injury-related catalyst — looks like public-driven narrative betting, not sharp action.

**Stake:** 0.8 units on Edwards at the best available price. If the line has moved past Edwards +200, the value is worth a half unit play. If Edwards is shorter than +130 anywhere, pass.

**Target line:** Edwards +130 is the floor for value here given the uncertainty of a rematch with minimal camp adjustment time.

**Data note:** Both fighters have solid UFC samples, though the weight-miss history on both sides (3 for Edwards, 5 for Dumont) and the extremely short turnaround means this analysis carries more uncertainty than a standard booking. Neither fighter's recent splits are particularly clean for trend-reading — their shared last fight showed suppressed volume from both sides.

**Market read:** No reverse line movement confirmed. The move toward Dumont appears public-driven — she lost six days ago and is now a -200 favorite in the rematch. That's the kind of narrative overcorrection that historically offers value on the other side.

**Hedge verdict: NO ARB.** Market data shows both fighters' lines moving in the same direction (both shortened), which means the combined implied probability has almost certainly crossed well over 100%. No cross-book arb opportunity exists here. Take the value pick on Edwards at the best available price.

**Props:** A "fight goes to decision" prop would carry value if available at reasonable odds — with Dumont's 8-fight decision streak and her 0% UFC finish rate, and Edwards' own pattern of grinding close decisions, the structural read strongly favors another judges' verdict. Check the lines against your book's vig before committing.

- Her five-fight winning streak includes wins of varying quality: a KO over a durable Cachoeira, a submission over Cornolle (who had beaten her before), and a decision over Dumont just six days ago. The momentum is real and recent.
<!--END-->

<!--F2_PROFILE-->
## Norma Dumont -- Style & Profile

- Dumont is one of the cleaner technical strikers in the women's bantamweight division, landing at 50% accuracy while absorbing only 2.33 strikes per minute — her 64% strike defense is among the best in the weight class. She wins by not getting hit, not by overwhelming opponents.
- Eight decisions in her last eight fights tell you everything about her style: she's a point-fighter who controls range, picks her shots, and frustrates opponents into grinding losses. She has zero submission attempts across her entire UFC career and zero KO wins.
- The caveat to her clean defensive numbers is that her output has been declining. She landed 2.13/min against Aldana in September 2024, dropped to 1.13 against Vieira, and then managed just 0.40 against Edwards in the fight six days ago — a fight she lost. Her last performance was her worst statistically.
- At 35 years old with five career weight misses, Dumont carries real risk around weight cuts and their downstream effects on her sharpness and output. Five misses is a significant track record of body-weight management problems.
- Her grappling is underrated — 54% takedown accuracy and 71% TD defense means she can both threaten on the feet without worrying about being taken down easily. She doesn't use wrestling offensively in recent fights (0.0 TD/15 in her last three) but she has the tools.
- She already lost to Edwards once, six days ago, and now has to come back and fight her again on a week's notice. The psychological and physical reset from a decision loss on that timeline is a genuine concern that the stats alone don't fully capture.
<!--END-->

<!--HEAD2HEAD-->
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

This fight already happened six days ago, and Edwards won a unanimous decision. The rematch question is whether Dumont can make meaningful adjustments in less than a week — and the honest answer is probably not. Edwards has a 3-inch reach advantage and is the more physically active fighter, and in their first meeting, Dumont couldn't find an answer for it. Both fighters managed just 0.40 significant strikes per minute in that bout, which tells you the pace was deliberately low, and in a low-output technical fight, Edwards' aggression and reach gave her the edge on the cards.

Dumont is the better pure technician on paper — her 64% strike defense is significantly cleaner than Edwards' 50%, and if she can establish range and use movement to force Edwards into missed exchanges, she can steal rounds on the judges' cards. Her path to winning this rematch runs through staying disciplined, not getting caught reaching, and using her footwork to make Edwards miss early. The problem is she couldn't execute that in fight one, and at 35 on six days of recovery from a loss, expecting a fundamentally different version of Dumont is optimistic.

Edwards' clearest advantage beyond the reach differential is her finishing ability. Dumont has never been stopped in her UFC career, which limits the KO/sub paths, but Edwards doesn't need to finish — she just needs to do what she already did. She pressures forward, lands cleaner in exchanges, and her 5-inch age advantage means the physical output gap that existed in fight one is unlikely to close in a week. The net intangible picture here slightly favors Edwards: she's younger, she already solved this matchup once, and she's shown finishing upside if Dumont slows down.
<!--END-->

<!--ENDINGS-->
## Most Likely Fight Endings

**#1 -- Joselyne Edwards wins by unanimous decision**
Likelihood: High
Why: The most recent data point we have is Edwards winning by unanimous decision over this exact opponent six days ago. Neither fighter has ever finished the other, Dumont has never been stopped in the UFC, and both are operating on minimal recovery time. A second gritty, technical decision with Edwards controlling the pace and reach advantage is the most logical projection.

**#2 -- Norma Dumont wins by split or unanimous decision**
Likelihood: Moderate
Why: Dumont is the more technically sound striker, and close fights go to the judges. If she makes minor adjustments — staying more active on the outside, using her footwork to control range, and forcing Edwards to chase — she can steal two rounds on a close card. Their first fight was clearly competitive enough that a second judges' verdict going the other way isn't far-fetched.

**#3 -- Joselyne Edwards wins by TKO/KO**
Likelihood: Low
Why: Edwards has real finishing power and can end fights when opponents slow down, as she showed against Cachoeira. Dumont has never been stopped in the UFC, but if the weight cut, the short turnaround, and the physical load of fight one accumulate, she could be more hittable than usual in the later rounds. It's not a likely outcome but it's in play given Edwards' proven finishing ability.
<!--END-->

**Pick: Joselyne Edwards**
Confidence: 57%

**The line:**
The market has moved significantly toward Dumont — she opened around +195 and is now sitting near -200, while Edwards went from +170 to +165. That's a massive shift on Dumont without confirmed reverse line movement driving it. The public appears to be reacting to a rematch scenario by assuming Dumont will correct the loss, which is a common narrative trap. Edwards won the fight six days ago and is now being offered as a moderate underdog.

**The case:**
Edwards solved this matchup once already. She has a 3-inch reach advantage, is five years younger, outputs at a higher rate, and has finishing ability Dumont simply doesn't possess. Dumont's output in fight one was the worst of her last three fights (0.40 SLpM), and she's coming back on six days' rest after a loss. The line moving almost 400 cents toward Dumont on a rematch she just lost — without an obvious sharp or injury-related catalyst — looks like public-driven narrative betting, not sharp action.

**Stake:** 0.8 units on Edwards at the best available price. If the line has moved past Edwards +200, the value is worth a half unit play. If Edwards is shorter than +130 anywhere, pass.

**Target line:** Edwards +130 is the floor for value here given the uncertainty of a rematch with minimal camp adjustment time.

**Data note:** Both fighters have solid UFC samples, though the weight-miss history on both sides (3 for Edwards, 5 for Dumont) and the extremely short turnaround means this analysis carries more uncertainty than a standard booking. Neither fighter's recent splits are particularly clean for trend-reading — their shared last fight showed suppressed volume from both sides.

**Market read:** No reverse line movement confirmed. The move toward Dumont appears public-driven — she lost six days ago and is now a -200 favorite in the rematch. That's the kind of narrative overcorrection that historically offers value on the other side.

analysis_sections.f1_profile:
## Rafa Garcia -- Style & Profile

- Garcia is a grinder who wins by volume and persistence rather than power. His career 4.28 strikes landed per minute is respectable, but his finishing rate tells the real story — seven of his last ten wins have gone to the scorecards, and he's the type who methodically builds advantages over 15 minutes rather than hunting the finish.
- His wrestling is his actual weapon. A 3.22 takedown average and 77% takedown defense are standout numbers at lightweight, and his last three fights show a fighter who knows exactly what he is: he wrestles when he needs to control the fight and doesn't take unnecessary risks on the feet.
- His striking last three fights is unusually suppressed — landing under 2.0 strikes per minute in each of those bouts — but his accuracy has improved (50–54% in two of three), suggesting he's throwing less and picking his spots more. The output is down, the efficiency is up.
- He knocked out Jared Gordon in Round 3 in September, which is notable because Gordon has one of the more durable chins in the division. Garcia showed he has stopping power when his opponents tire, not because he's a natural KO artist.
- The KO loss to Grant Dawson in 2024 (Round 2, early finish) is the one crack in the armor. He was taken down, put on his back, and stopped — a reminder that if he loses the wrestling battle, he's vulnerable to top pressure.
analysis_sections.f2_profile:
## Alexander Hernandez -- Style & Profile

- Hernandez is a streaky fighter whose career has operated in distinct phases — he goes on runs of two or three wins and then falls back. He knocked out Chase Hooper and Diego Ferreira in back-to-back fights before running straight into Garcia and losing a clear decision.
- His strike defense at 59% career average is below average for lightweight, and he absorbs 4.45 significant strikes per minute — essentially the same rate he dishes out. He's been in firefights his entire career, which has accumulated wear over 27 professional fights.
- The KO losses to Billy Quarantillo and the submission loss to Renato Moicano represent two different vulnerability patterns: he's been stopped on the feet and on the ground. Against Garcia specifically, he went to zero takedowns over three rounds and landed just 0.53 strikes per minute — completely neutralized.
- His recent KO wins over Hooper and Ferreira came in short bursts, not as a result of sustained pressure. Against Chase Hooper he landed 3.42 strikes per minute but finished late in Round 1; against Ferreira he barely landed (1.37/min, 33% accuracy) but caught him with one clean shot in Round 2. He's a momentum fighter — when he's rolling, he's dangerous, but he doesn't win grinding wars.
- At 33 years old with a weight miss on record and a history of absorbing punishment at a high rate, there are real questions about whether his chin and gas tank will hold up against a fighter who specializes in exactly the pace and pressure Garcia applies.
- His takedown defense at 68% is the softest number in this matchup — below the threshold where Garcia's wrestling becomes genuinely decisive.
analysis_sections.head2head:
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

This is a rematch, and the first fight was one-sided enough to create a real puzzle for the betting market. Garcia won a unanimous decision over Hernandez just six days ago, controlling the bout with his wrestling and never letting Hernandez get comfortable on the feet. For Hernandez to reverse that result, he needs a fundamentally different game plan — and there's no evidence from his recent fight history that he has one available. His takedown defense at 68% is below the level that would let him keep Garcia standing, and Garcia is precise enough to pick his shots on the feet while setting up level changes. The reach advantage Hernandez holds (72" vs. 70") is real but marginal, and it didn't help him in the first fight.

The path for Garcia looks similar to Fight 1: mix up the striking with wrestling, keep Hernandez working backward, and let the accumulated pace pressure erode whatever early momentum Hernandez can generate. The path for Hernandez runs through a specific window — catching Garcia in an exchange before Garcia establishes his wrestling rhythm, ideally in the first round or two. Hernandez has genuine pop in his hands, as Hooper and Ferreira found out. If Garcia gets overconfident coming in as the short-priced favorite and stands in front of Hernandez early, there's a real knockout sitting there. But that window gets smaller with each minute Garcia controls the pace, and Hernandez has shown no ability to stop Garcia's takedowns.

The intangibles don't tilt much either direction here — both men are coming off a fight six days ago, so there's no layoff advantage to exploit. The line movement tells its own story: Garcia opened as the underdog at +130 and has been bet down to -105, while Hernandez has moved the opposite direction from +105 to -150. That kind of sharp movement toward a fighter who just *lost* to his opponent six days ago is attention-grabbing, and it demands an explanation before accepting Garcia as the value side. One possibility: Garcia's low output in the first fight (0.67 strikes per minute) gives the books and sharp bettors a different view of where the damage actually landed. Another is that fight camp adjustments or a weight-cut narrative have leaked into the market.
analysis_sections.endings:

**#1 — Rafa Garcia wins by Unanimous Decision, Round 3**
Likelihood: High
Why: Garcia's wrestling is the controlling variable here, and with Hernandez unable to stop a single takedown in Fight 1, there's no structural reason to expect a different outcome. Garcia doesn't need a finish — he grinds out 15 minutes of wrestling-heavy MMA and wins on all three scorecards the same way he did last week.

**#2 — Alexander Hernandez wins by KO/TKO, Round 1 or 2**
Likelihood: Moderate
Why: This is Hernandez's only realistic path. He has one-shot knockout power and Garcia, coming in as the short-number favorite with a win already in hand, could be caught if he tries to trade early instead of immediately establishing his grappling. Hernandez finished Hooper and Ferreira inside two rounds both; if Garcia gives him space and clean looks early, it happens fast.

**#3 — Rafa Garcia wins by TKO, Round 3**
Likelihood: Low
Why: Garcia finished Jared Gordon — a durable veteran — in Round 3 of their September fight after wearing him down. If Hernandez's gas tank starts leaking in the championship round, Garcia could turn up the volume late. The accumulated damage from a second consecutive fight in six days could be a factor for Hernandez's chin.

**#4 — Alexander Hernandez wins by Unanimous Decision, Round 3**
Likelihood: Low
Why: Possible only if Hernandez adjusts his footwork and range management enough to avoid the wrestling for three full rounds, which he failed to do in Fight 1 with full fight camp preparation. The scoring would need to go his way on the feet, which requires sustained output he hasn't shown against Garcia.
analysis_sections.betting:
**Tier: NO BET**

**Primary Pick: PASS**

The line movement on this fight is the dominant factor and it points in a direction that creates genuine uncertainty. Garcia beat Hernandez six days ago by unanimous decision, opened as a +130 underdog for the rematch, and has since been bet to nearly even money at -105. Meanwhile Hernandez — the man who just lost — has moved from +105 to -150. A 255-point swing in the opposite direction of what the result would suggest is not public money. That's sharp action telling a story, and it's not a story that can be confidently overridden by what happened six days ago.

The statistical read favors Garcia clearly. His wrestling advantage is real, Hernandez's 68% takedown defense is soft enough to be exploitable, and the first fight wasn't close. An assessed win probability for Garcia in the high 50s to low 60s feels defensible on the data alone. But the de-vigged market is now pricing Hernandez at roughly 60% — meaning the market is saying Garcia's actual chance is closer to 40%. That's a gap wide enough to flag as potential value on Garcia, but not when the line has moved this sharply, this fast, this soon after a fight where the same two men already competed.

The problem with betting Garcia at -105 is that the market disagrees with that read in a way that warrants caution, not conviction. Sharp money at this scale on the loser of a fight that finished six days ago typically means something is known that isn't in the public data — a weight cut issue with Garcia, a camp adjustment from Hernandez's team, or injury information that hasn't surfaced. Without knowing what's driving the move, the edge that appears on paper carries more uncertainty than the numbers suggest.

**Stake: 0 units — passing this fight**

**Target line:** Garcia would be a play at +120 or better if the market data weren't this contradictory. At current prices, there is no clean bet.

**Data tiers:** Garcia — sufficient UFC sample, recent activity, no missing core stats. Hernandez — same. Both have enough data to analyze; the issue is the market signal, not the data.

**Market read:** Garcia opened +130, current -105. Hernandez opened +105, current -150. Heavy movement toward Hernandez — the fighter who lost six days ago — without reverse line movement classification in the raw data, but the magnitude and direction are sharp enough to demand respect. No public percentage data available to confirm public/sharp split.

**Hedge verdict: NO ARB.** With Garcia at -105 and Hernandez at -150, the combined implied probability is approximately 48.8% + 60.0% = 108.8% — well above 100%. No arb structure exists. Take the value pick on the better line if betting, but given the market conflict, the recommendation is to pass entirely.

**Props worth considering:** A "fight goes to decision" prop would align with both fighters' profiles — Garcia grinds, Hernandez didn't land a takedown and barely struck in Fight 1. However, the implied probability of a decision is so high given both fighters' recent patterns that the prop line would need to be checked against actual market pricing before any value could be identified. Without live prop lines, no specific prop recommendation is available.

**Final note:** The honest answer on this rematch is that the fight itself has a clear analytical direction (Garcia's wrestling wins again) but the market is priced in a way that makes acting on that direction irresponsible with the available information. Pass and wait for a cleaner spot.

analysis_sections.f1_profile:
## Davey Grant -- Style & Profile

- At 40 years old, Grant is ancient by bantamweight standards, and his output reflects it. His last-3 SLpM average of roughly 2.0 is less than half his career mark of 5.58 — he is not the volume striker his UFC averages suggest. He's fighting conservatively, surviving on accuracy and ring IQ rather than pace.
- His striking accuracy has actually held up (49% and 60% in his last two wins), which tells you he's picking shots carefully rather than throwing freely. When he does commit, he lands — he's just committing far less often.
- Defensively, his 54% career strike defense is mediocre, and he allowed Charles Jourdain to submit him in Round 1 in his most recent loss before this rematch. He's been submitted and knocked out in his career, so there are openings in every phase.
- He has no takedown activity at all in his last three fights (0.0 TD/15 across the board), which means he's operating purely as a striker in short exchanges. Whatever wrestling game he had on paper hasn't shown up recently.
- His fight history tells the story of a journeyman who can still pull off a good performance — wins over Assuncao and Martinez show he can compete — but the trajectory is clearly downward. He's 40, slowing down, and coming off a fight six days ago.
- The 6-day layoff is the dominant context here. He's coming directly off a 15-minute fight against this exact opponent. Whatever he showed in that fight, Luna Martinetti has seen all of it — and Grant is recovering from it, not preparing fresh.
analysis_sections.f2_profile:
## Adrian Luna Martinetti -- Style & Profile

- Luna Martinetti's career UFC averages are eye-catching for all the wrong reasons: 8.80 SLpM landed but 9.30 absorbed, with only 47% strike defense. In theory, that's a high-volume exchange artist who bleeds in both directions. In practice, his last two fights look nothing like that — 1.2 and 2.87 SLpM in his UFC appearances, suggesting those career numbers come from a very limited sample of exactly two fights.
- With only two UFC fights on record, almost everything we know about him statistically is thin. His win over Vologdin was a decision at 2.87 SLpM, and he lost to Grant at 1.2 SLpM. The career averages are dominated by those two fights and should not be treated as settled truths about who he is.
- The 100% takedown defense is technically accurate per the data but comes from a two-fight UFC sample — treat it as noise, not a reliable signal. He attempted zero submissions across both fights as well.
- He's 30, at the peak of his physical prime, which is a meaningful contrast to his 40-year-old opponent. He should have more recovery capacity, more athleticism, and less accumulated wear on his body heading into this rematch.
- He lost a unanimous decision to Grant six days ago in the fight immediately preceding this one. That is the most important data point in the entire matchup — he has already fought this man, lost a full three-round decision, and is now rematching on six days' notice on the same card. He has seen what Grant does. So has Grant.
- The line has moved sharply in his favor since opening, suggesting the market believes the rematch dynamic favors Luna Martinetti — whether because of adjustments, the belief that Grant is more drained, or simply recency reaction to an upset-style result.
analysis_sections.head2head:
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

This is an unusual fight to analyze because we have direct evidence: Grant beat Luna Martinetti by unanimous decision six days ago, in the fight immediately before this one on the same card. That's not a fight from 18 months ago with camp adjustments in between — that's the same night. Grant's pace in that fight was 2.93 SLpM with 49% accuracy; Luna Martinetti managed just 1.2 SLpM at 44%. Grant controlled the fight on output and accuracy. Both fighters are now being asked to do it again before either has had a proper recovery window.

The key structural question in the rematch is whether Luna Martinetti, at 30 years old and ten years Grant's junior, recovers faster from a full 15-minute fight than a 40-year-old does. Grant's recent output is already trending well below his career average — he's at roughly a third of his career SLpM in his last few fights — and taking another full three-round fight six days later compounds that. If Grant's gas tank was running at 70% in Fight 1 due to age and accumulated ring miles, it's reasonable to expect him at 60% or less in Fight 2. Luna Martinetti's path to winning this fight runs almost entirely through volume and pressure in the later rounds, assuming Grant fades faster the second time around.

That said, Grant won the first fight with disciplined, accurate striking rather than volume — he doesn't need to throw a lot to win, and Luna Martinetti hasn't shown the ability to stop the fight by finish. Neither fighter has been active on the mat in these fights, so this is a pure kickboxing match at low-to-moderate pace. analysis_sections.f1_profile:
## Raoni Barcelos -- Style & Profile

- At 39, Barcelos has reinvented himself as a pure grappling tactician. His last three fights all went the distance via decision, and in each one his output was strikingly low — averaging under 1.0 significant strikes per minute — while his takedown and submission activity did the scoring work. His 0.4 SLpM against Garbrandt and 0.2 against Jackson in their first meeting tell you everything about his game plan: he is not here to brawl.
- His takedown defense is genuinely elite at 87% career, and it has been the backbone of his run. Against wrestlers, he's nearly impossible to hold down; against strikers, he gets to dictate whether the fight stays standing or goes to the mat on his terms.
- He has won five straight fights, including quality scalps in Cody Garbrandt, Ricky Simon, and Jackson himself. All five went to decision — zero knockouts or TKOs allowed — and each win was built on controlling pace, clinching, and grinding out scoring rounds rather than flashy exchanges.
- The age flag is real. Barcelos turns 40 this year, and his striking output has declined dramatically in recent fights — from a career average of 4.65 SLpM down to well below 1.0 in his last three. Whether that's intentional game-planning or physical decline is the key question, but the pattern is clear: he is doing far less work offensively than his career numbers suggest.
- Two weight misses in his history point to a frame that has outgrown the 135-pound class. His fight pace suggests he may be managing the cut carefully, which compounds the age-related fitness concerns if a rematch turns into a volume war.
analysis_sections.f2_profile:
## Montel Jackson -- Style & Profile

- Jackson's calling card is his elite defensive shell — a 1.40 strikes absorbed per minute career average that is exceptional for a 135-pounder, built on his Southpaw stance, 75-inch reach, and disciplined head movement. When he is operating at his best, he is nearly untouchable at range.
- That 8-inch reach advantage over Barcelos is the largest structural weapon in this matchup. It should theoretically allow him to fire and disengage before Barcelos can tie him up — but the first fight proved he did not exploit it effectively, posting just 0.93 SLpM and allowing Barcelos to close distance and wrestle.
- His three first-round knockouts (Blackshear in 18 seconds, Yahya in under four minutes, Strader in under two) show genuine power when he gets clean looks, but those wins came against opponents who walked into his counters. Against wrestlers who cut off distance before he can load up, that power hasn't shown up — his four losses have all come to fight-shapers who took away his range.
- Jackson's takedown defense sits at 67% career, which is a legitimate vulnerability. He shot zero takedowns in both of his recent losses, and when Barcelos controlled clinch distance in their first fight, Jackson offered no counter-wrestling answer and nothing to reclaim range or scoring initiative.
- Two consecutive split-decision losses heading into this rematch — first to Barcelos, then to Deiveson Figueiredo — and his output has cratered in that stretch: 0.27–0.93 SLpM across those three fights. His striking efficiency in that loss to Figueiredo (0.53 SLpM, 40% accuracy) suggests he is struggling to generate offense when opponents are willing to be cautious with him.
analysis_sections.head2head:
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

These two already fought five days ago and Barcelos won a split decision — so the central question of this rematch is not who has the stylistic edge in theory, but whether Jackson can make the adjustments he failed to make the first time around. The structural problem for Jackson is the same one it was in fight one: Barcelos's 87% takedown defense neutralizes Jackson's ability to force action, and Jackson's 67% takedown defense means Barcelos can threaten the mat whenever he wants. Jackson's massive reach advantage reads well on paper, but against a fighter who does not exchange at range and instead commits to closing distance and clinching, that reach becomes largely irrelevant. Barcelos took the fight to the inside in fight one, nullified Jackson's best weapon, and won three rounds of positional grinding.

Jackson's path to winning this rematch runs entirely through his output and his willingness to engage first. He needs to use his footwork to stay at the end of that 75-inch reach, punish Barcelos on the way in, and not allow the clinch to become the default position. He posted zero takedowns in fight one — whether by choice or inability — and that passivity cost him rounds. If he fights the same way a second time, he loses the same way. His power is real when he gets clean looks, but he has to be willing to initiate and absorb some risk to create those looks. The issue is that his last three performances show a fighter trending toward passivity, not aggression.

The net intangible picture does not shift the needle dramatically. Both fighters are coming off a fight literally six days ago, so the fatigue and damage question applies to both equally. Barcelos is five years older at 39, which is a real concern, but his fight style — low-output grappling control, not explosive athleticism — ages better than an elite athlete who relies on burst and reflexes. Jackson is the younger man, but younger men who fight passively do not benefit from their physical advantages. Barcelos won the first meeting, he is fighting from confidence, and the rematch dynamic typically favors the fighter who imposed their style in fight one.
analysis_sections.endings:
## Most Likely Fight Endings

**#1 -- Barcelos wins by decision, Round 3**
Likelihood: High
Why: This is exactly how fight one ended, and nothing in the structural matchup or recent trajectory suggests fight two unfolds differently. Barcelos closes distance, limits Jackson's range work, scores takedown threats and clinch control for round credit, and grinds out a third consecutive unanimous or split decision win over a fighter who has not shown the adjustments needed to stop it. Jackson's passivity in the last three fights makes a high-volume counter-performance unlikely.

**#2 -- Jackson wins by decision, Round 3**
Likelihood: Moderate
Why: If Jackson arrives with a different approach — staying disciplined at range, using his reach to pot-shot Barcelos on entries, and avoiding the clinch — he has the defensive profile to frustrate Barcelos for fifteen minutes and take a close decision. He lost fight one on the margins, and judges rewarding striking over wrestling control in a rematch on a short turnaround is a real possibility. This outcome requires Jackson to fight smarter, not just harder.

**#3 -- Jackson wins by KO/TKO**
Likelihood: Low
Why: Barcelos has not been stopped by strikes in his last several fights and brings reasonable durability to this rematch. However, Jackson has legitimate knockout power at bantamweight, and a rematch against a 39-year-old on a six-day turnaround creates some vulnerability if Barcelos's output drops further and he gets caught coming in. It would require clean, flush contact — something Jackson rarely generated in fight one — but his first-round KO history keeps this non-trivial.

**#4 -- Barcelos wins by submission**
Likelihood: Low
Why: Barcelos has a submission in his last five wins and 0.7 attempts per 15 minutes career. If he gets extended top control in round three against a tiring Jackson, a rear-naked choke or guillotine is within his game. His sub attempt rate in the first meeting was 1.0 per 15 minutes, so he was looking for it. Against a fighter with no submission losses on record it remains unlikely, but it is part of his arsenal.

However, Jackson has legitimate knockout power at bantamweight, and a rematch against a 39-year-old on a six-day turnaround creates some vulnerability if Barcelos's output drops further and he gets caught coming in. It would require clean, flush contact — something Jackson rarely generated in fight one — but his first-round KO history keeps this non-trivial.

**#4 -- Barcelos wins by submission**
Likelihood: Low
Why: Barcelos has a submission in his last five wins and 0.7 attempts per 15 minutes career. If he gets extended top control in round three against a tiring Jackson, a rear-naked choke or guillotine is within his game. His sub attempt rate in the first meeting was 1.0 per 15 minutes, so he was looking for it. Against a fighter with no submission losses on record it remains unlikely, but it is part of his arsenal.

**Recommendation: LEAN — Barcelos**
Confidence: 58%

Barcelos won fight one six days ago and the rematch odds have moved heavily in Jackson's favor — opening at +180, he is now a -221 favorite, while Barcelos has shortened from +186 to +150. That is a dramatic line shift that reflects either sharp conviction that Jackson makes the necessary adjustments or heavy public action on the narrative of a revenge fight. The problem is the first fight is the data. Barcelos imposed his game plan cleanly, Jackson posted zero takedowns, and the structural mismatch — Barcelos's 87% takedown defense against Jackson's 67% takedown defense — has not changed in six days.

The line movement here does not show reverse line movement; the public and the market both moved toward Jackson, which is the expected narrative bet on an athletic 34-year-old getting a quick rematch chance. This does not carry the same sharp-money signal that reverse line movement would. Given that, the stat-based read holds weight: Barcelos at +150 is being offered at a price that implies roughly a 40% win chance on a fighter who just beat the same opponent five days ago by outgrappling him for three rounds.

The age and weight miss history are real flags on Barcelos — he is 39, has missed weight twice, and his output has cratered in recent fights. The six-day turnaround also concerns both fighters equally. These factors prevent a strong play. - **Stake:** 1.5 units

- **Both fighters' data:** Barcelos has five recent UFC fights with full splits; Jackson has recent activity including the first meeting. Both carry enough data to work with, though Jackson's intangibles history is listed as unavailable.

This appears to be narrative-driven action on the rematch story rather than a confirmed sharp-money signal. No reverse line movement is present, so there is no reason to override the stat-based read.

Converting to implied probabilities: -221 implies approximately 68.8%, and +150 implies approximately 40.0%, for a combined implied probability of roughly 108.8%. analysis_sections.f1_profile:
## Ryan Spann -- Style & Profile

- Spann is a pressure-forward fighter whose best weapon is his submission game — he's finished three of his last five wins by sub, most of them in under three minutes of Round 1. When he gets his hands on someone, bad things happen quickly for the other guy.
- His striking is volume-limited (3.11 sig. strikes/min career) and his accuracy sits at just 39%, which means he's not a high-percentage boxer — he's more of a threat when he closes distance and drags the fight to the mat or the clinch.
- The chin is a real concern. He's been stopped by KO/TKO in three of his last six fights (Guskov R2, Cortes-Acosta R2, and before that Smith and Krylov). He absorbs 3.92 significant strikes per minute, materially more than he lands, and his strike defense at 43% is poor.
- He took a clear step up in activity level against Brzeski last July — 5.39 takedowns per 15 minutes and a Round 1 submission — then looked flat against Cortes-Acosta before turning it around in this first meeting with Buchecha. His output is volatile, but his grappling when it clicks is genuine finishing-level.
- At 34, with accumulated damage visible across his recent film, Spann is not a fighter you want to bet on absorbing clean power shots. His path to winning almost always runs through the takedown or clinch — when he stands and trades, he tends to lose.
- Layoff of just six days heading into this rematch, which is a flag: this is an extremely compressed turnaround and both men are running it back almost immediately.
analysis_sections.f2_profile:
## Marcus Buchecha -- Style & Profile

- Buchecha is one of the most decorated grapplers ever to enter MMA — a multiple-time ADCC champion and BJJ legend — but his UFC career tells a sobering story. In nine fights he has zero submissions, zero knockouts, and zero decision wins. The grappling dominance that defined him in pure BJJ has not translated cleanly to MMA.
- His striking output is extremely low: 1.59 sig. strikes/min career, and in the Nzechukwu draw he was landing less than one significant strike every eight minutes. He fights at a slow pace and rarely initiates exchanges with urgency.
- His takedown accuracy is alarmingly poor at 16% — he's shooting, but he's not getting them. His takedown defense is recorded at 0%, which is almost certainly a sample-size artifact, but it does signal he's been taken down at will when opponents choose to use the threat.
- In his last three fights he has not finished anyone and has gone 0-2-1. He lost a decision to Martin Buday — a fighter with no finishing power — and could only draw with Kennedy Nzechukwu. That's not a grappler threatening elite competition; that's a grappler who is struggling to impose himself on journeyman-level opponents in a striking sport.
- At 36, he is past the window where most grapplers make their adjustments and break through. His striking has not developed meaningfully and his power game on the feet remains negligible (0.56 sig. strikes/min in the first Spann fight).
- He has now been knocked out clean by Spann, which adds a meaningful layer of concern heading into the rematch — both for how he absorbs punishment and for the mental/physical reset required in just six days.
analysis_sections.head2head:
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

The first fight between these two already happened, and it ended with Spann stopping Buchecha by TKO in Round 2. That result is the loudest data point in this analysis. Buchecha landed fewer than one significant strike per minute, shot takedowns at 16% accuracy, and was overwhelmed by Spann's physical pressure once the exchanges started. The result wasn't a fluke — it reflected a real structural gap between a grappler who hasn't developed MMA-functional striking and a fighter whose raw athleticism and submission threat can overwhelm opponents before a five-round survival fight develops.

Spann's path to winning a second time runs through the same blueprint: pressure Buchecha against the cage, smother the takedown attempts with his own physicality (he has a reach and frame advantage), and look for submissions or TKO opportunities when Buchecha is forced to cover up on the feet. Buchecha's only realistic path to reversing the result is either landing a takedown that sticks — something he's done very rarely, especially against opponents who defend aggressively — or surviving long enough for Spann's cardio and volume inconsistency to open a window. Given it's a three-round fight, that window is narrow. Buchecha would need to drag this into a grinding late-round exchange where Spann has clearly fatigued, and his recent track record doesn't suggest he has the offensive output to capitalize even if that scenario materializes.

The net intangible picture is fairly even — both fighters carry the same six-day turnaround, and both are dealing with the physical toll of their recent encounter. The difference is that Buchecha is the one absorbing the KO loss and returning on extremely short rest, which historically is a measurable disadvantage. Fighters returning from a stoppage loss in their immediately preceding fight — especially one that came in Round 1 or early Round 2 — tend to show caution and hesitation in the rematch. Buchecha's already-limited offensive output could shrink further in the early rounds as he adjusts to being on the wrong end of the first result.
analysis_sections.endings:
## Most Likely Fight Endings

**#1 — Ryan Spann wins by TKO/KO, Round 1 or 2**
Likelihood: High
Why: The first fight ended this way and nothing structurally has changed in six days. Buchecha's striking remains passive, his takedown accuracy is too low to control where the fight takes place, and Spann's pressure and finishing instincts are genuine. A fighter returning from a stoppage loss on minimal rest is a meaningful fade, and Buchecha is walking into an opponent who already knows exactly how to break him.

**#2 — Ryan Spann wins by submission, Round 1 or 2**
Likelihood: Moderate
Why: Spann's three-fight submission streak before the Cortes-Acosta loss showed he can still finish on the mat, and Buchecha — despite his BJJ credentials — has been submitted before and has never submitted anyone in MMA. If Buchecha does succeed in getting the fight down, Spann has shown the scrambling ability and submission threat to reverse positions and threaten his own finish.

**#3 — Fight goes to a decision, Spann wins**
Likelihood: Low
Why: If Buchecha successfully neutralizes Spann's early pressure and keeps the fight on the feet at range, the pace of both men is slow enough that this could grind into a decision. Spann would still likely win on volume and threat variance, but this outcome requires Buchecha to fight a much cleaner, more defensively disciplined fight than he has in any of his last three outings.

**#4 — Marcus Buchecha wins by decision**
Likelihood: Low
Why: The only path here is Buchecha successfully using takedowns to control position without finishing — which is his entire UFC career on paper, yet he still couldn't get a decision win against mid-tier competition. For this to happen against Spann, who has 1.21 takedowns per 15 minutes of his own and submission threats from his back, Buchecha would need a performance dramatically above his established ceiling.
analysis_sections.betting:
## Betting Recommendation

**PASS — No bet on the moneyline.**

Here's the honest problem with this fight from a betting standpoint: it already happened. Spann beat Buchecha by TKO six days ago, and now the line has completely flipped — Buchecha opened as the underdog at +136 and has moved to -110 in the rematch. That's a dramatic market overcorrection in a direction that is very hard to justify with the available data.

The line movement here is not driven by sharp fight analysis — there is no reverse line movement, and the movement toward Buchecha is almost certainly a combination of: action from Buchecha's camp and entourage who believe he got caught in a bad spot, recreational money on the "revenge fight" narrative, and possibly some book adjustment to balance handle. Nothing in Buchecha's actual fight data supports installing him as the even-money pick or slight favorite coming off a stoppage loss on six days' rest.

Spann at -110 (or close to even) is an interesting number given he just knocked this man out, but there are real reasons to hesitate. His chin has been cracked multiple times in recent years, his striking defense is poor, and if Buchecha lands anything clean in this rematch Spann can go. The six-day turnaround for both men is genuinely concerning from an analytical standpoint — we have no idea what either fighter's physical condition looks like heading in.

The core issue is this: the current market data shows lines that are essentially dead-even (both near -110), and while the statistical and historical case clearly favors Spann, the data confidence on a six-day rematch is genuinely low. Both fighters' intangible situations are compromised, and the first-fight result has already been absorbed into some form of market narrative rather than clean price discovery. The honest stake math on an essentially coin-flip line against a fighter who just won doesn't produce actionable value — it would require a larger edge gap than what exists when both sides are sitting near even money.

**Primary Pick: PASS**
Confidence: Not sufficient for a play at near-even lines
Stake: 0 units

**Target line for a Spann play:** If Spann were available at -130 or shorter (i.e., the market hadn't overcorrected this far), there would be a legitimate lean given the recency of his finish and Buchecha's inability to produce offense. At near-even money, the book's margin eats the edge.

**Data context:** Buchecha has only 9 UFC fights and his record contains significant gaps — his profile carries lower confidence. Spann's data is more complete but his inconsistency and chin history introduce real variance.

**Market read:** The line moved from Spann -110 / Buchecha +136 to essentially even (-110 both ways). This is substantial movement toward Buchecha without a clear sharp signal behind it — more likely narrative-driven public action. There is no confirmed reverse line movement. In a case like this, the movement away from Spann on no new information is a mild fade signal *toward* Spann, but not enough to overcome the data concerns at even money.

**Hedge verdict:** NO ARB. With both fighters near -110, the combined implied probability is approximately 109-110%, meaning the book's vig makes any two-sided play a guaranteed loser. There is no arbitrage opportunity here.

**Props worth considering:** If a "fight ends inside the distance" prop is available and priced reasonably, there is a genuine case for it. Spann finishes fights, Buchecha has not gone past Round 2 with Spann before, and neither man is a pace-grinding decision hunter. A finish prop at anything below -150 would carry value on the historical evidence — but without access to live prop lines, no specific number can be confirmed as actionable.

analysis_sections.f1_profile:
## Eric McConico -- Style & Profile

- McConico is a low-volume, defensive-minded fighter whose game plan revolves around survival and activity rather than finishing. His career striking output of 2.92 per minute is modest, and in his last three fights that number has dropped even further — he landed just 0.73 strikes per minute in the first Vieira fight, suggesting he's comfortable winning ugly.
- He has a meaningful reach advantage at 77 inches, four inches longer than Vieira, and as a southpaw he creates additional angle problems for orthodox opponents. Against someone who shoots low and often, that reach can help him establish distance and pick off opponents on the way in.
- His takedown defense at 68% is serviceable but not elite — good enough to frustrate most wrestlers occasionally, but not a wall. He held Vieira to 4 takedowns per 15 minutes in their first meeting rather than zero, meaning Vieira did get in, he just couldn't do enough once there.
- The KO loss to Nursultan Ruziboev (round 2, 33 seconds) and the later stoppage by Baisangur Susurkaev (round 3) are real concerns. Two knockout losses in four fights, including one brutal early finish, suggest his chin has been tested and found unreliable under sustained power. The Susurkaev KO came in round 3, which has more cardio-fade implications than chin, but combined with the Ruziboev stoppage the picture isn't clean.
- His southpaw stance is a genuine asset here — Vieira's recent run of opponents has not included a southpaw among the last five fights on record, meaning the left-hand lead and mirror footwork will be unfamiliar angles for a wrestler who already struggles on the feet.
- The 6-day layoff is unusual and the context matters: this appears to be a rematch booked on short turnaround. With no intangibles data available, the short gap between fights is a flag worth noting even if it applies equally to both men.
analysis_sections.f2_profile:
## Rodolfo Vieira -- Style & Profile

- Vieira built his UFC career entirely on his jiu-jitsu. Four of his last ten wins are submissions, and his grappling lineage is legitimate — he's a multiple-time IBJJF world champion. When he gets the fight to the ground and in a controlled position, he's a credible finishing threat even against experienced grapplers.
- The problem is getting there. His takedown accuracy of 22% across his UFC career is genuinely poor for someone whose entire game depends on wrestling. He shoots often — 2.87 attempts per 15 minutes — but lands less than a quarter of them. In the first McConico fight, he attempted more and hit 4 per 15 minutes, but that may reflect McConico's willingness to engage in scrambles rather than Vieira's execution improving.
- On the feet, Vieira absorbs punishment at an alarming rate. His career striking defense sits at 49%, and he eats 4.28 significant strikes per minute — one of the higher absorption numbers in the division. The Bo Nickal KO came in round 3 and followed a pattern where Vieira spent rounds getting hit while unable to close the distance. He has now been knocked out twice, both times in round 3, which points to a cardio problem compounding whatever chin issues exist.
- Two KO losses in recent memory — Nickal in round 3 and now a pattern of late-round deterioration — signal that when his wrestling doesn't pay off early, Vieira has no reliable backup. He doesn't counter-punch, he doesn't create distance, and he keeps walking forward into shots when tired.
- At 36, with accumulated damage from a grinding UFC run, his body is not getting younger and his ability to sustain a high-wrestling pace for three full rounds is questionable, especially given the two late-round stoppage losses on his ledger.
analysis_sections.head2head:
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

This rematch is essentially a rerun of a structural problem Vieira couldn't solve the first time: he needs to get McConico down and hold him there long enough to work submissions, but McConico's reach, southpaw angles, and 68% takedown defense make that path harder than Vieira's record suggests it should be. In their first meeting, Vieira did manage takedowns — but he couldn't finish from them, and McConico stayed active enough on the feet during standup exchanges to win rounds. The southpaw angle is particularly significant here. Vieira has no recent experience against lefties, and that stance shift disrupts the standard entry lines that a wrestler relies on. The lead-leg timing, the angle of the jab, the position of the power hand — all of it is mirrored, and Vieira's striking defense (49% career) gives McConico plenty of opportunities to land clean behind that four-inch reach advantage.

The key variable is whether Vieira's cardio holds. Both of his knockout losses have come in round 3, which is a consistent signal that when he can't finish the grappling puzzle early, he deteriorates. McConico is not a finisher — zero stoppages in his last four fights — so this fight almost certainly goes three rounds. That's the worst scenario for a Vieira whose engine runs out late. McConico does have his own knockout losses to account for, but the Susurkaev stoppage also came in round 3 and had the feel of a cardio fade rather than a clean knockout over the chin. Against a fighter who isn't a natural power striker, McConico may survive the back half of this fight just fine.

The net intangible picture is close to neutral — both men are 6 days out from the first fight and both are on the wrong side of 35. McConico holds a slight edge simply by virtue of having won the first fight and having the more favorable style on the feet. Vieira needs a different answer this time, and there's no evidence in his recent film that he has one.
analysis_sections.endings:
## Most Likely Fight Endings

**#1 — Eric McConico wins by unanimous decision, Round 3**
Likelihood: High
Why: This is how the first fight ended and the structural conditions haven't changed. McConico controls range with his reach advantage and southpaw angles, keeps the fight competitive on the feet, and survives Vieira's grappling attempts without being finished. Vieira's late-round cardio problems mean McConico likely wins the third round going away, same as last time.

**#2 — Rodolfo Vieira wins by submission, Round 1 or 2**
Likelihood: Moderate
Why: Vieira's only realistic path is an early grappling finish before McConico's reach and Vieira's own fatigue become decisive. If Vieira can chain takedowns more effectively in the opening two rounds and find a back take or guillotine opportunity, his submission pedigree is real enough to close it out. He's done it six times in UFC competition — he just needs to do it before the third round.

**#3 — Rodolfo Vieira wins by TKO, Round 3**
Likelihood: Low
Why: McConico has been stopped twice, once late, and if Vieira somehow maintains enough output to land clean shots in the championship round while McConico's own gas tank empties, a late stoppage isn't impossible. However, Vieira's own history of fading in round 3 makes this a low-probability outcome requiring both fighters to have their roles reverse from the first fight.

**#4 — Eric McConico wins by split or majority decision**
Likelihood: Low
Why: If Vieira has a better second round through improved wrestling and McConico has to work harder to escape, the scorecards tighten. One or two judges seeing the grappling volume as dominant could split the decision, even if the overall outcome still goes to McConico.

analysis_sections.f1_profile:
## Jackson McVey -- Style & Profile

- McVey is a pure striking-forward aggressor who throws at an extremely high volume — 6.51 significant strikes per minute career average, and in the first Dumas fight he was at 9.4/min. He attacks constantly and does not fight cautiously.
- His striking accuracy is elite for the UFC (69% career), which means the volume isn't desperation — he's connecting. Against Dumas in April, he landed at 72.4%, confirming that accuracy holds even when he's throwing in bunches.
- His grappling defense is essentially non-existent on paper — 0% takedown defense recorded in his UFC run — but the absence of takedowns against him may reflect that opponents haven't attempted them consistently rather than confirmed vulnerability.
- He has been finished twice by submission in his last three fights (Ferreira in R1, Reese in R2), which is a real pattern worth noting. Both losses came against dedicated grapplers who got him to the mat, suggesting his submission defense once taken down is the primary exploitable hole in his game.
- His submission wins (and 1.2 sub attempts per 15 min career) show he can threaten from the bottom or in scrambles himself — this isn't a one-dimensional striker who goes limp on the ground.
- At 27 and southpaw, he presents an awkward angle for orthodox opponents, and that 77-inch reach at 6'4" lets him operate at range. His southpaw lead hand and longer frame make his jab and left hand naturally threatening against a right-handed opponent.
analysis_sections.f2_profile:
## Sedriques Dumas -- Style & Profile

- Dumas is a slow-output decision fighter whose game is built on conservative movement and grinding decisions — his three UFC wins all went the distance, and he averages under 2 significant strikes per minute for his career. He does not impose himself on fights offensively.
- His recent form has fallen off a cliff. In his last three outings he's been submitted twice (by Johnson in R2, then by McVey in R1) and there was a no-contest before that. He hasn't registered a win since Denis Tiuliulin in October 2024, and the losses have been genuinely bad — getting submitted in under 90 seconds twice in a row is a serious alarm.
- He has been knocked out cold in two of his last five UFC losses — Oleksiejczuk got him in R1, Ruziboev got him in R1 — both by fighters known for power. He has been stopped in the first round by both strikes and submissions in recent memory, which means there are multiple ways to finish him and he hasn't shown the ability to survive adversity.
- His takedown defense is 33%, which is among the worst in the division. He's attempted 1.30 takedowns per 15 minutes career but only lands 31% of them, so his wrestling is more of a threat than an execution. In the last three fights his TD output was 2.34 in one fight and 0.0 in the other two — highly inconsistent.
- His strike defense (53%) looks acceptable on paper, but against McVey's volume it proved irrelevant — he couldn't stay upright long enough to use it, absorbing McVey's pace and losing by submission inside two and a half minutes.
- At 30, he's entering an age window where the repeated early stoppages and accumulated damage start compounding. He has been finished four times in his last five losses, three of them in round one.
analysis_sections.head2head:
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

The most obvious fact about this matchup is that it already happened six days ago — McVey submitted Dumas in round one at 2:14. That isn't a data point to be weighed alongside others; it is the defining piece of evidence. McVey's striking volume overwhelmed Dumas on the feet, and when the fight went to the ground it ended quickly in McVey's favor. Dumas's low output (1.34 strikes per minute in that fight, 75% accuracy on a tiny sample) confirms he never got anything going offensively.

The structural problem for Dumas is that McVey attacks in a way that neutralizes everything Dumas does reasonably well. Dumas's path to winning fights has historically been surviving and outworking opponents over three rounds on the scorecards — but McVey's volume at 6-9 strikes per minute makes surviving three rounds against him enormously difficult. Dumas's 33% takedown defense means he can't reliably use wrestling to change the fight's location, and even if he does get McVey down, McVey has shown he can threaten from bottom position with submission attempts of his own. Dumas, by contrast, has zero submission attempts per 15 minutes in his career average and didn't attempt any in the first fight.

The only realistic path for Dumas is landing something clean on the feet early — he does have two first-round KO losses on his resume that show opponents with power can be dangerous to him, but McVey has zero KO wins and isn't the type to end fights with a single punch. Dumas needs to either slow McVey down with his 79-inch reach advantage or survive into a place where McVey's volume creates scrambles Dumas can exploit. That's a thin scenario given what just happened between these two, and there's nothing in Dumas's recent record suggesting he's capable of executing it.
