analysis_sections.f2_profile:
## Assuerio Silva -- Style & Profile

- Assuerio Silva is 51 years old and has not competed professionally in approximately 19 years — his last recorded fight was April 2007 against Cheick Kongo. This is not a long layoff. This is effectively a different lifetime. Whatever his physical attributes and skills were in 2007 bear no meaningful relationship to what he is capable of in 2026.
- His UFC career record shows a fighter who absorbed punishment at a brutal rate — 4.20 significant strikes absorbed per minute, with only 54% strike defense. In three consecutive UFC losses, he was finished by submission, out-struck badly, and outworked over decisions. He was not competitive in those fights.
- His grappling numbers from that era showed some takedown activity (2.66 per 15 minutes) but with a deeply poor 34% accuracy and 25% takedown defense — numbers that were already mediocre nineteen years ago.
- His two UFC wins, both in 2005, came via first-round finishes — a KO in 11 seconds and a submission in under three minutes. He was dangerous in short bursts against lesser opposition nearly two decades ago. There is no credible path to projecting that version of him onto a 51-year-old man returning from a 19-year absence.
- Any statistical analysis of Silva's "current" profile is essentially fiction. His last-three fight splits are from 2006 and 2007. His record, his reach, his intangibles — none of this reflects a fighter who exists in any competitive sense in 2026.
analysis_sections.head2head:
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

This matchup requires an unusual degree of directness: the data does not describe a competitive fight. Michelle Montague is an active 32-year-old professional who went three hard rounds with Mayra Bueno Silva in April 2026 — a ranked UFC strawweight. Assuerio Silva is a 51-year-old heavyweight who last fought in April 2007, before many current UFC fighters were adults. The physical, athletic, and competitive gap here is not a question of style or statistics — it is categorical.

On the stylistic level, Montague's wrestling-first approach would theoretically be tested by a larger, physically strong opponent with a grappling background. Silva does carry heavyweight dimensions at 241 pounds, which is a significant size advantage on paper. But size advantages require the underlying athlete to be functional, conditioned, and recently active. A 51-year-old returning from nineteen years away from competition is not going to leverage a size advantage against an active, conditioned professional fighter. Montague's path to winning is simple and reliable: control the pace, use her activity and conditioning advantage, and either take the fight down or simply out-work him over three rounds. Silva has no realistic path to winning that doesn't involve a very early, very lucky strike — and even that assumes a level of explosive power that simply cannot be assumed from a 51-year-old who hasn't trained competitively in nearly two decades.

The net intangible picture is as lopsided as the fight. Montague is coming off a recent UFC win. Silva carries the largest layoff penalty imaginable — nineteen years is not a factor any analytical framework is built to account for, because no framework anticipates a competitor at this age and this level of inactivity being booked against an active professional. If this is a legitimate sanctioned contest, Montague should win, and the only genuine uncertainty is how quickly she finishes it versus how long Silva's size keeps him from being finished cleanly.
analysis_sections.endings:

Likelihood: High
Why: Her two UFC wins have both gone the distance, and Silva's size may make a clean finish difficult. Montague is patient and methodical — she's likely to control the fight through wrestling and activity, accumulate rounds, and win a comfortable decision without needing the finish.

Likelihood: Moderate
Why: A 51-year-old returning from nineteen years away is unlikely to absorb clean shots well. If Montague finds the right exchange early, the fight could end quickly. Silva's career numbers already showed he absorbed 4.20 significant strikes per minute against active heavyweights — he is not a defensive fighter.

Likelihood: Low

If the fight goes to the ground — likely, given her wrestling — she may opt for control and ground-and-pound rather than submissions, but a rear-naked choke or similar from a dominant position remains a non-zero possibility.

analysis_sections.f1_profile:
## Cody Durden -- Style & Profile

- Durden is primarily a grappler who uses volume wrestling to grind out decisions, but his striking output has cratered lately — he averaged just 0.73 strikes per minute in the first meeting with Filho and 0.27 against Tumendemberel. He wins by control, not damage.
- His takedown rate (3.99 per 15 minutes career) is the engine of everything he does, but accuracy at 49% means he's shooting and missing a lot — he earns his takedowns through volume, not precision.
- The recent fight history tells a concerning story: five losses in his last seven, with two KO losses (Bruno Silva, Jose Ochoa) and two submissions on top of that. He's been finished in multiple ways by multiple fighters at this level.
- At 35, he's at the age where flyweights typically start declining, and the Ochoa KO (stopped in 11 seconds of Round 2) is a red flag — he's been put away by a non-elite striker, which is not a chin you want to bet on in a rematch.
- His one recent positive result was the April 2026 decision win over Filho himself, so he does hold a proven blueprint against this specific opponent. That result matters — he outworked Filho for three rounds with wrestling and movement.
- Southpaw stance is a structural wrinkle, but with only 50% strike defense and a deteriorating record against live competition, the stance advantage is the thinnest part of his case here.
analysis_sections.f2_profile:
## Jafel Filho -- Style & Profile

- Filho is a submission specialist first and foremost — his 1.8 submission attempts per 15 minutes career average is the dominant statistical signature, and four of his last seven wins have come by submission or KO, all finished in Round 1.
- The problem is he almost never gets there against opponents who can wrestle. Both of his decision losses (Nascimento, Durden) came against grinders who controlled pace and neutralized his offense — he's averaged 0.2–0.73 SLpM across those three losing performances, essentially invisible offensively.
- His striking output (1.78 SLpM career) is among the lowest in the flyweight division, and his 40% accuracy suggests he's not a volume puncher in any sense — he's waiting for scrambles and submission entries, not creating offense on the feet.
- When he does get the fight to the mat on his terms, he's genuinely dangerous — three straight first-round sub finishes before the Durden loss show legitimate finishing ability, but that sequence came against Carpenter, Osbourne, and Barez, none of whom are elite competition.
- His takedown defense is 50%, which means wrestlers who commit to the shot are getting him down more often than not — a critical liability against Durden specifically.
- Coming off the April 2026 loss to Durden where he managed just 0.2 strikes per minute and 1.0 takedown per 15 minutes, the blueprint for shutting him down is already on film and already proven.
analysis_sections.head2head:
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

The structural read on this matchup is straightforward: Durden is the busier wrestler with proven finishing ability against Filho specifically, while Filho is the more dangerous finisher but only when he controls the scramble game. The key variable is takedown defense — Filho's sits at 50%, meaning Durden has historically gotten him down with regularity. In a wrestling-based flyweight fight, that number is decisive. Durden doesn't need to hurt Filho; he just needs to shoot, drag him down, and work top control for 15 minutes, which is exactly what he did six days ago.

The glaring context here is that this is a same-week rematch. Durden already beat Filho by unanimous decision on April 25, 2026 — the most recent fight in both fighters' records. That result is not a historical data point to weigh against other fights; it is the definitive current read on this matchup. Durden outworked Filho in every round, and nothing in Filho's profile suggests he has a new answer. Filho's path to winning requires either a submission off a scramble or a late-fight collapse from Durden — but Durden's decision wins are generally clean (he's gone the distance multiple times without being stopped by fatigue), and his grappling volume makes him hard to submit off the bottom since he's rarely the one getting taken down.

The line movement is the most interesting part of this analysis. Filho opened at +500 and has been bet all the way down to -400 — a massive, sustained move. Durden opened at +330 and has lengthened to +400. That means the sharp money has hammered Filho heavily enough to move an opening underdog line by 900 cents to a heavy favorite. This is not public action driving a famous name — Filho is not that kind of public draw. This is the market being repriced by informed money, which almost certainly reflects either new information (camp performance, injury to Durden, conditioning concerns) or a strong consensus read that the first fight was a fluke or a stylistic aberration. Given that Durden already showed signs of durability problems (the Ochoa stoppage, the Bruno Silva KO) and is 35 years old, the sharp side may be pricing a meaningful degradation in his performance over six days of turnaround on an aging, previously-hurt body.
analysis_sections.endings:
## Most Likely Fight Endings

**#1 — Jafel Filho wins by submission, Round 1 or 2**
Likelihood: Moderate
Why: Filho's only realistic path to victory runs through a scramble submission, and the extreme line move suggests informed money believes the short-turnaround is genuinely compromising Durden's ability to execute his wrestling game. If Durden is physically limited from the April 25 fight, Filho gets a scramble entry he didn't get last time. His first-round sub rate against opponents he catches is well-documented.

**#2 — Cody Durden wins by unanimous decision**
Likelihood: Moderate
Why: This is literally what happened six days ago. Durden's wrestling volume kept Filho from generating anything offensively, and three rounds of grinding control work produced a clean scorecard. If the rematch follows the same template — and nothing in Filho's kit has changed in less than a week — Durden repeats the result.

**#3 — Jafel Filho wins by unanimous decision**
Likelihood: Low
Why: Filho has shown he can win decisions when his grappling entries work (he outworked Nascimento in a similar grind), but his 0.2 SLpM performance against Durden in the first fight makes it hard to construct a scoring path. This outcome requires Durden to be noticeably compromised and Filho to be sharper — possible on a six-day turnaround dynamic, but not the base case.

**#4 — Cody Durden wins by submission**
Likelihood: Low
Why: Durden does have submission wins (Matt Schnell, one career sub in last 10), but his 0.7 sub attempts per 15 minutes is low. Against Filho's active guard, getting a submission would require extended top control, which he demonstrated he can achieve — but converting it is a different matter.

The sharp money is clearly telling us something that isn't visible in the public stats.

Here's the problem from a betting standpoint: Durden's +400 line implies roughly a 20% win probability. His last-3 stats, taken at face value, show he just beat this exact opponent. But the market doesn't care about that — it's pricing in something on the Durden side (six-day turnaround at age 35, his prior KO losses, potential wear from the first fight) and something on the Filho side (a finisher who only needs one scramble to win). Against confirmed sharp money of this magnitude, betting Durden at +400 carries genuine risk even if the paper matchup looks favorable.

On the other side, Filho at -400 means you're risking four units to win one. Even if you believe Filho wins 70% of the time in this rematch, the -400 price only offers value at roughly 80%+ true probability — and the first fight result makes 80% a hard number to justify.

**Assessed probability on Filho:** approximately 68–72% (reflecting both the sharp line move and the first-fight result as a competing signal). At -400, that's not enough.
**Assessed probability on Durden:** approximately 28–32%. At +400, that's close to break-even value but the sharp disagreement is too significant to fade aggressively.

**Hedge verdict: NO ARB.** Best available Durden line (+400) and Filho line (-400) produce a combined implied probability of approximately 120%, making a guaranteed-profit hedge impossible. The vig on this fight is enormous given the line differential.

**Props worth considering:** If you must act on this fight, a "Filho wins inside the distance" prop may carry value if priced anywhere near -130 or longer, given his four finishes in last six wins and the possibility that a fatigued, short-turnaround Durden gives up a scramble he controlled last week. But without prop line access, no specific recommendation can be made.

The honest answer is that this rematch, on six days' rest, with the market doing something extraordinary to the line, is not a fight to force a position on. Pass.

Here's the problem from a betting standpoint: Durden's +400 line implies roughly a 20% win probability. His last-3 stats, taken at face value, show he just beat this exact opponent. But the market doesn't care about that — it's pricing in something on the Durden side (six-day turnaround at age 35, his prior KO losses, potential wear from the first fight) and something on the Filho side (a finisher who only needs one scramble to win). Against confirmed sharp money of this magnitude, betting Durden at +400 carries genuine risk even if the paper matchup looks favorable.

On the other side, Filho at -400 means you're risking four units to win one. Even if you believe Filho wins 70% of the time in this rematch, the -400 price only offers value at roughly 80%+ true probability — and the first fight result makes 80% a hard number to justify.

**Assessed probability on Filho:** approximately 68–72% (reflecting both the sharp line move and the first-fight result as a competing signal). At -400, that's not enough.
**Assessed probability on Durden:** approximately 28–32%. At +400, that's close to break-even value but the sharp disagreement is too significant to fade aggressively.

**Stake: 0 units**

analysis_sections.f1_profile:
## Francis Marshall -- Style & Profile

- Marshall is primarily a grappler who wins by controlling where the fight takes place. His 2.71 takedowns per 15 minutes and 88% takedown defense are the two numbers that define him — he gets the fight down when he wants it and keeps it standing when he doesn't.
- His striking output is genuinely low (3.19 strikes/min career, and even lower in his last three fights), but he's shown improved accuracy in recent outings — 56% in the Brennan rematch and 60% against Silva — which is a meaningful jump from his career 34% baseline.
- The submission attempt against Erik Silva (6.04 per 15 minutes, finished in 1:29 of Round 1) shows he can be explosive on the ground when the opportunity is there, though that rate is an outlier inflated by a short fight.
- His losses tell a consistent story: he struggled against Gomis on the feet, got knocked out by Dulgarian in Round 1, and lost a close split to Santos. He's beatable when opponents keep the fight standing and are physically capable of threatening him off the cage.
- The KO loss to Dulgarian is worth noting — he was stopped cleanly in Round 1 by a fighter with genuine power, which signals some vulnerability when hurt early. That said, he's had no such issues since and has won his last two fights comfortably.
- Against Brennan specifically, Marshall went 3 rounds, controlled the grappling exchanges, and won every frame despite a very low output night — suggesting he can win ugly and doesn't need highlight-reel moments to take rounds on the judges' cards.
analysis_sections.f2_profile:
## Lucas Brennan -- Style & Profile

- Brennan has exactly one UFC fight on record — a unanimous decision loss to Marshall — which makes any deep statistical analysis impossible. His career UFC averages are drawn entirely from that one fight, so treat them as a snapshot, not a trend.
- What that snapshot shows is bleak: 0.13 significant strikes landed per minute, 25% accuracy, zero takedowns attempted, and 33% takedown defense. He was largely unable to impose any offense across 15 minutes.
- He is a southpaw, which is a real structural variable against orthodox fighters, but Marshall handled the stance matchup without apparent difficulty in the first meeting, so the advantage is essentially neutralized in a rematch context.
- His one submission attempt per 15 minutes (career) suggests he may have some grappling ambitions from the bottom, but zero takedown attempts of his own tells you he's not going to be the one initiating grappling exchanges — he needs the fight to stay standing.
- The weight miss on his record is a flag. With only one UFC fight in the books and already a weight cut issue flagged, there are real questions about whether 155 is the right home for his body long-term, and whether that contributed to a flat performance against Marshall.
- His pre-UFC record (11-2 before his UFC debut) presumably tells a fuller story, but that context isn't available here — we're working almost entirely blind on what makes Brennan dangerous when he's performing at his ceiling.
analysis_sections.head2head:
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

This is a rematch where the first fight provided a clear answer: Marshall controlled the contest from the opening bell with his wrestling, Brennan generated almost nothing offensively, and the unanimous decision was not particularly close in terms of who was dictating the action. Marshall's 88% takedown defense makes it nearly impossible for an opponent to change the location of the fight without his consent, and Brennan at 33% takedown defense simply cannot stop Marshall from taking the fight where he wants it. The grappling dynamic completely favors Marshall, and Brennan showed no ability to solve it over three full rounds.

Where Brennan would need to win is on the feet, and the numbers suggest he struggled to land anything meaningful even when given space. Marshall is not a high-volume striker, but 3.19/min versus Brennan's 0.13/min in their actual meeting is a production gap that goes beyond one bad night — Brennan was actively unable to generate offense while defending Marshall's wrestling. The southpaw stance is real edge in theory, but in a rematch against a fighter who already solved it, its value diminishes significantly. Brennan would need to come in with demonstrably different tools, and there's no evidence in the available data that he has them.

The net intangible picture slightly favors caution about Marshall — he did absorb a clean Round 1 KO loss to Dulgarian earlier in his run, and his output numbers are quite low — but against this specific opponent, none of that is particularly actionable. Brennan's weight miss history and the fact that he was held to near-zero offense in the first fight are the more pressing concerns. The primary question for this rematch is not whether Marshall has weaknesses (he does), but whether Brennan has shown any evidence he can exploit them. The first fight suggests he cannot.
analysis_sections.endings:
## Most Likely Fight Endings

**#1 -- Francis Marshall wins by unanimous decision, Round 3**
Likelihood: High
Why: The first fight ended this way, and the stylistic dynamic hasn't changed. Marshall uses his wrestling to control position, keeps Brennan from generating offense, and wins rounds on volume control even when the striking output from both men is low. Judges reward the fighter dictating where the fight happens, and that's Marshall.

**#2 -- Francis Marshall wins by submission, Round 1 or 2**
Likelihood: Moderate
Why: Marshall finished Erik Silva in under 90 seconds with a rear-naked choke, and if Brennan's takedown defense (33%) breaks down early in a round, Marshall has shown the ground-and-pound-to-submission pathway. A second fight sometimes sees the dominant wrestler pressing harder for the finish once the blueprint is already established.

**#3 -- Lucas Brennan wins by decision**
Likelihood: Low
Why: This would require Brennan to either solve the wrestling puzzle he couldn't solve across 15 full minutes in the first fight, or outwork Marshall on the feet consistently across all three rounds. Marshall's low striking output means there's a version of this fight where an aggressive Brennan could outpoint him, but his own numbers from the first meeting make this extremely difficult to envision.

analysis_sections.f1_profile:
## Victor Valenzuela -- Style & Profile

- The data available on Valenzuela from UFC records is essentially a single fight — a 47-second submission loss to Edson Berto in June 2007. That is nearly 19 years ago. Any career averages derived from that one appearance (100% striking accuracy, 0% strike defense) are statistical noise from a sub-one-minute fragment, not a meaningful profile.
- What we do know from the broader record: he is 13-6-2 across his career, suggesting a fighter with legitimate experience, but the UFC context is so thin and so stale that it tells us almost nothing about who he is today.
- His most recent fight — which is the only piece of current-era data we have — is the decision win over Max Griffin on April 25, 2026. That result is directly relevant to this rematch and is the single most useful data point available.
- The line movement toward Valenzuela (opening -110, now -150) suggests the market has a stronger read on him than the raw stats indicate — likely informed by the fight tape from their first meeting, which ended in Valenzuela winning a unanimous decision.
- Without height, reach, age, or stance data, and with UFC statistical history limited to one ancient fragment, this profile relies heavily on the outcome of Fight 1 and on the line movement as a proxy for informed opinion.
analysis_sections.f2_profile:
## Max Griffin -- Style & Profile

- At 40 years old, Griffin is on the back end of a long UFC run. His last three fights are all losses — a submission to Michael Chiesa, a split decision loss to Chris Curtis, and a unanimous decision loss to Valenzuela in the fight this rematch follows. That's a three-fight losing streak heading into a rematch with the man who just beat him.
- His striking output has cratered in the last three fights. Career average is 3.70 significant strikes per minute, but his last three fights show 0.25, 1.40, and 0.67 — a collapse in offensive volume that strongly suggests either physical decline, stylistic adjustment problems, or both. A fighter who used to throw nearly four strikes a minute is now barely touching one in recent outings.
- His strike defense sits at 58% for his career, which is workable, but he is absorbing 3.69 significant strikes per minute over his career — nearly matching what he dishes out. He is not a fighter who hides from damage, and at 40, accumulated wear is a real concern.
- He has 69% takedown defense, which is below the threshold where strikers can reliably dictate where a fight goes. He has attempted almost no takedowns in his last three fights (0-1-0 TD per 15 min across those outings), meaning he is essentially fighting as a pure striker without the grappling threat to keep opponents honest.
- Six days layoff is listed as his time since last fight — but his last fight is April 25, 2026 against Valenzuela, which is the fight we're now rematching. That either reflects a data artifact or an unusually short turnaround. If this fight is genuinely happening within days of their first meeting, that is a significant red flag for Griffin's physical readiness. If it's a data formatting issue and adequate time has passed, the three-fight losing streak and the already-happened loss to this exact opponent remain the primary concern.
- Griffin's wins in recent memory came against Jeremiah Wells (split decision), Tim Means (split decision), Carlos Condit (decision), Song Kenan (KO), and Ramiz Brahimaj (KO). The KO wins show he still has finishing power, but those came in 2022, and the more recent output suggests that punch resistance and output have both declined significantly.
analysis_sections.head2head:
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

The most important piece of context for this fight is that it has already happened once. Valenzuela beat Griffin by unanimous decision in their April 2026 meeting, and Griffin's output in that fight — 0.67 significant strikes per minute at 41.7% accuracy with a single takedown attempt — was his worst offensive performance in the three fights we have splits for. He was outworked, outlasted, and unable to impose anything. That result is not a data point to be explained away; it is the clearest signal we have about how this matchup functions.

Griffin's main realistic path back into this fight is his power. He has genuine KO ability — two stoppages in his last ten fights, including a first-round finish — and if he can land a clean shot early, the outcome changes instantly. But to land that shot, he needs to produce volume, and his volume in recent fights has been troublingly low. He absorbed significant pressure from Curtis and was thoroughly controlled by Valenzuela. At 40, with three straight losses and a body that has taken considerable UFC mileage, asking him to suddenly perform at a higher level than his last three outings requires more evidence than is available. Valenzuela, meanwhile, has already shown he can manage Griffin across three rounds, and the line move in his direction suggests the market agrees that his approach translates well against this particular opponent.

The net intangible picture heavily favors Valenzuela. Griffin is 40 years old, on a three-fight losing skid, with a documented volume collapse over his last three appearances — and his most recent fight was already a loss to this exact man. Valenzuela has the stylistic edge confirmed by the only head-to-head data we have. Unless Griffin shows up as a fundamentally different fighter, the structure of this rematch looks very similar to the original: Valenzuela outworks him on the feet, controls pace, and wins another decision if it goes the distance — or catches a tired, declining Griffin late.
analysis_sections.endings:
## Most Likely Fight Endings

**#1 -- Victor Valenzuela wins by decision**
Likelihood: High
Why: He already beat Griffin this way, and nothing in the data suggests Griffin has solved the problems that led to that loss. Griffin's offensive output has been historically low in his last three fights, and Valenzuela's pace and pressure produced a clean unanimous verdict in Fight 1. A rematch following the same pattern — Valenzuela outworking a 40-year-old Griffin over three rounds — is the most straightforward outcome.

**#2 -- Max Griffin wins by KO/TKO**
Likelihood: Moderate
Why: Griffin still carries real power, with two stoppages on his record in recent years and a first-round KO against Song Kenan. If Valenzuela gets reckless or walks into something clean, Griffin can end a fight quickly. This is his only realistic path to winning — landing a fight-changing shot before pace and volume catch up with him.

**#3 -- Victor Valenzuela wins by submission or TKO**
Likelihood: Low
Why: Valenzuela has shown submission ability historically, and Griffin was tapped out by Michael Chiesa in their most recent fight before this rematch — suggesting he is not impossible to submit. If Valenzuela adds a grappling dimension to his game plan or catches a fatigued Griffin late, a finish is possible, even if not the expected route based on Fight 1's clean decision result.

Griffin is 40 years old, has lost three straight including one to this exact opponent, and his offensive output over his last three fights is roughly 18% of what his career average would suggest. There is no data point available that argues Griffin has corrected whatever allowed Valenzuela to control him last time.

- Current line: Valenzuela -150 (implied probability approximately 60.0%; de-vigged approximately 57.7%)

The analysis leans on the April 2026 head-to-head result and the line movement as the primary inputs. Griffin's data is complete and recent, and it paints a consistent picture of decline. The thin data on Valenzuela is acknowledged — this is not a high-confidence swing, it is a measured play on the one concrete result we have (their prior meeting) and the line that reflects informed market opinion.

**Flip conditions:** If Griffin's layoff is genuinely only six days from the first fight (suggesting this is a very short turnaround *for Griffin as well* and we simply lack Valenzuela's comparable layoff data), or if there is fight tape showing Valenzuela was significantly more competitive than the scorecard suggests and nearly got finished, this pick deserves reconsideration. A line move past -180 on Valenzuela before fight time would also signal the value is gone.

**Market read:**
The line has moved approximately 40 cents toward Valenzuela (from -110 to -150) with no reverse line movement. That is straightforward sharp action on Valenzuela. The market is agreeing with the stat-based read, which provides modest additional confidence.

**Hedge verdict: NO ARB**
With live market odds unavailable for both sides simultaneously, a formal arb calculation cannot be completed. Based on the available BestFightOdds line movement data alone, there is no indication of cross-book divergence sufficient for an arbitrage play. Take the value on Valenzuela at the best available -150 line and do not hedge.

**Props:**
A "fight goes to decision" prop has theoretical value here given Griffin's recent inability to finish anyone and his low output against Valenzuela in Fight 1 — but without a de-vigged prop line available, no specific prop play can be recommended. The directional lean toward a decision is noted; evaluate if a prop line is available at your book.

analysis_sections.f1_profile:
## Talita Alencar -- Style & Profile

- Alencar is a grinder who wins by making fights miserable rather than spectacular. Her career striking rate of 3.09 significant strikes per minute is modest, and her 36% accuracy reflects a fighter who isn't trying to set up power shots — she's clinching, controlling, and dragging opponents into deep water.
- Her grappling is the engine of everything she does. She averages 3.01 takedowns per 15 minutes with a 66% takedown defense rate, which means she can get fights to the mat on her terms and largely prevent opponents from doing the same to her.
- She's exceptionally patient and doesn't get hurt. In her last six fights, she has zero knockout losses and zero KO wins — every finish either goes to the scorecards or ends via submission, which tells you this is a woman who grinds through adversity rather than cracking under it.
- Her last three fights all went the distance, and she's won all three. More tellingly, she beat Polastri just a day ago by unanimous decision, going to a third round she had the conditioning for. She absorbs 3.67 significant strikes per minute, which is notably more than she lands, but her 51% strike defense and willingness to stay in close quarters suggest she manages that deficit through positioning, not head movement.
- At 35 years old in the strawweight division, the age clock is real, but nothing in her recent performance indicates a measurable decline — her last three fights are wins, and her conditioning held across three consecutive 15-minute decisions.
- One legitimate concern: her striking output in her last fight against Polastri dropped to 0.8 significant strikes per minute, her lowest recent number. That's a fighter who was content to control rather than engage — which works if the judges reward it, but risks point-scoring issues on a repeat.
analysis_sections.f2_profile:
## Julia Polastri -- Style & Profile

- Polastri is a volume striker with legitimate pop. She lands 6.22 significant strikes per minute over her UFC career at 56% accuracy — those are elite numbers for the division. When she's firing, she's one of the more dangerous strikers at 115 pounds.
- The problem is she's not always firing. Her last three fight outputs tell three completely different stories: 4.1 strikes per minute in a KO win over Kowalkiewicz, 0.93 against Godinez in a loss, and just 1.4 against Alencar in her most recent loss. That kind of variance isn't a style adjustment — it's a sign that opponent pressure and grappling disruption completely derails her offense.
- She has a massive five-inch reach advantage over Alencar, which should theoretically let her operate from the outside and land before Alencar can get close. The fact that she didn't use it effectively in their first fight is significant.
- Her takedown defense sits at 57%, which is the core vulnerability in this matchup. Against a fighter who attempts takedowns at Alencar's frequency, that number means she's getting taken down regularly.
- At 28, she's physically in her prime, and the KO win over Kowalkiewicz — a recognizable name — showed her ceiling when she's allowed to work at range and her output is flowing. That's the best version of Polastri, and it's genuinely dangerous.
- She's coming off a loss to the woman she's about to fight again. The psychological reset matters, and whether she's made specific tactical adjustments to handle Alencar's grappling is the central unknown heading into this rematch.
analysis_sections.head2head:
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

These two already fought, and Alencar won unanimously. The blueprint from that fight tells you nearly everything you need to know about how the rematch shapes up. Polastri's striking volume — her primary weapon — was throttled to 1.4 significant strikes per minute, a fraction of her career average. Alencar's pressure and grappling disrupted Polastri's timing, kept her from setting her feet, and negated the reach advantage that should make Polastri dangerous. A 57% takedown defense rate means Polastri gets taken down roughly four times every 15 minutes against someone shooting at Alencar's rate — and once she's dealing with takedown threats, her strike output collapses entirely.

The reach gap is the counterargument. Polastri has five inches on Alencar, and if she can establish distance and use that range consistently, she has the tools to keep Alencar on the outside and accumulate damage before the takedowns arrive. Her career striking accuracy of 56% is impressive, and when she landed cleanly against Kowalkiewicz, she finished the fight. Alencar absorbs 3.67 significant strikes per minute and her strike defense is a mediocre 51% — Polastri has the firepower to make her pay if she can fight her fight. The problem is she couldn't fight her fight the first time, and nothing in the data suggests she's found a structural solution to the grappling problem in the span of one fight cycle.

The net intangible picture slightly favors Alencar. She's older at 35, which is a real consideration in this division, but Polastri is coming off a loss to this exact opponent and must now solve a problem she's already failed to solve once. Alencar's path to victory is replicating the first fight: stay close, neutralize the reach advantage by getting inside it, and drag the fight into a grinding third round where her conditioning and work rate win the cards. Polastri's path requires a genuine tactical shift — using her jab and footwork to stay at range, avoiding the fence, and not letting Alencar establish clinch control early. That's a reasonable adjustment on paper. Whether she's actually made it is the bet.
analysis_sections.endings:
## Most Likely Fight Endings

**#1 — Talita Alencar wins by unanimous decision, Round 3**
Likelihood: High
Why: This is exactly how their first fight ended, and the structural reasons it happened haven't changed. Alencar's pressure and grappling neutralize Polastri's striking advantage, the fight grinds through three rounds, and the judges reward Alencar's control and volume of work. Polastri's 57% takedown defense doesn't give her the tools to stop this pattern from repeating.

**#2 — Julia Polastri wins by KO/TKO, Round 1 or 2**
Likelihood: Moderate
Why: Polastri studied the first fight and knows precisely where her striking needs to land. If she uses the jab effectively to keep Alencar on the outside in the early rounds and catches her coming in, she has the finishing power to change the fight in a single exchange. Alencar takes 3.67 significant strikes per minute and has zero knockout wins in her career — she's not a devastating puncher who keeps opponents cautious. If Polastri finds her range early, this is her best route to a finish.

**#3 — Julia Polastri wins by unanimous decision, Round 3**
Likelihood: Low
Why: If Polastri adjusts her footwork, avoids getting backed against the fence, and consistently wins the striking exchanges at range, she can accumulate enough point-scoring activity to take the scorecards. Her career striking numbers are significantly better than Alencar's — the question is whether she can replicate that output against an opponent specifically designed to prevent it. It's possible but requires a cleaner tactical execution than she showed in fight one.
